title: When Women Trigger Each Other About Money
subtitle: A reading on the social dynamics of female comparison around wealth
excerpt: A reading on why women's financial success can trigger other women more than men's does, where the comparison mechanism comes from, and what the trigger is usually pointing at in the person experiencing it.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain literature on social comparison, female friendship dynamics, and money psychology.

practice.whenToUse: Read when comparison to other women's financial success is a recurring pattern and you want to understand the mechanism before working on it.

content.text: A reading on the social mechanics of financial comparison between women. The trigger is common enough to examine without shame: the feeling that another woman's win costs you something.

Social comparison research consistently shows that people compare more sharply with members of their own group than with members of distant groups. A woman who earns roughly what you earn is a more potent comparison point than a billionaire whose situation feels irrelevant to yours. Her win sits at just the right distance: close enough to feel adjacent, successful enough to feel like a measurement.
This is why a friend's business success can produce more discomfort than a news story about a stranger's wealth. The stranger is not in your reference group. Your friend is.

The triggered feeling often runs on an implicit model that money is finite in a way that makes one person's gain another's loss. This model is not conscious. Most people who experience the comparison trigger would, if asked, say that they know logically that her success doesn't affect their chances. The logic doesn't help because the model is running below the level of conscious reasoning.

The scarcity model often has roots in household experience — in places where money was limited in real terms and one person's having something did mean another person going without. The model was accurate in that context. It is being applied in a context where it doesn't fit.

The triggered feeling is usually most acute when something in your own money situation feels stuck, behind, or not moving at the pace you wanted. The trigger is not really about her — it is about the gap between where you are and where you want to be, which her win makes more visible.

This makes the trigger useful rather than embarrassing. It is pointing at something specific in your own situation. The practices in this section work on the pointed-at thing, not on eliminating the comparison response — which would require not caring about money at all.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain literature on social comparison, female friendship dynamics, and money psychology.
